rules.rule_statement: A promoter is a person who engages in activities, such as procuring capital and entering into contracts, to bring the corporation into existence as a business entity.
rules.short_rule_statement: A promoter is a person who engages in activities, such as procuring capital and entering into contracts, to bring the corporation into existence as a business entity.
rules.source_text:
Promoters
A promoter is a person who engages in activities, such as procuring capital and entering into contracts, to bring the corporation into existence as a business entity.

rules.rule_statement: A promoter is personally liable for knowingly acting on behalf of a corporation before incorporation. The promoter remains jointly and severally liable for those actions, even after the corporation comes into existence, unless a subsequent novation releases the promoter from liability. To establish liability, it is insufficient that a person should have known that the corporation was not formed. Actual knowledge of the entity’s pre- incorporation status is required. If a party who contracts with a promoter knows that the corporation has not yet been formed and agrees to look only to the corporation for performance, then the promoter is not liable. EXAM NOTE: Promoter liability is often tested in the context of pre-incorporation agreements.

Liability For Pre-Incorporation Agreements

The promoter remains jointly and severally liable for those actions, even after the corporation comes into existence, unless a subsequent novation releases the promoter from liability. To establish liability, it is insufficient that a person should have known that the corporation was not formed. Actual knowledge of the entity’s pre- incorporation status is required. If a party who contracts with a promoter knows that the corporation has not yet been formed and agrees to look only to the corporation for performance, then the promoter is not liable. EXAM NOTE: Promoter liability is often tested in the context of pre-incorporation agreements.

rules.rule_statement: A promoter stands in a fiduciary relationship with the pre-incorporated corporation. The promoter can be liable to the corporation for violating a fiduciary duty, such as by making a secret profit (e.g., failing to disclose a commission on a pre-incorporation transaction).

Fiduciary Duty To The Corporation
A promoter stands in a fiduciary relationship with the pre-incorporated corporation. The promoter can be liable to the corporation for violating a fiduciary duty, such as by making a secret profit (e.g., failing to disclose a commission on a pre-incorporation transaction).

rules.rule_statement: Although a promoter can seek compensation for pre-incorporation activities undertaken on the corporation’s behalf and reimbursement for related expenses, the promoter cannot compel the corporation to make such payments, because the promoter’s acts, although done to benefit the corporation, are not undertaken at the corporation’s direction.

Right To Reimbursement
Although a promoter can seek compensation for pre-incorporation activities undertaken on the corporation’s behalf and reimbursement for related expenses, the promoter cannot compel the corporation to make such payments, because the promoter’s acts, although done to benefit the corporation, are not undertaken at the corporation’s direction.

rules.rule_statement: A corporation is not liable for pre-incorporation transactions entered into by a promoter. The fact that the promoter entered into a transaction to benefit a future corporation is not sufficient to hold the corporation liable. Because a corporation is not necessarily in existence during a pre-incorporation transaction, a principal-agent relationship does not exist between the corporation and the promoter.

General Rule—No Liability
A corporation is not liable for pre-incorporation transactions entered into by a promoter. The fact that the promoter entered into a transaction to benefit a future corporation is not sufficient to hold the corporation liable. Because a corporation is not necessarily in existence during a pre-incorporation transaction, a principal-agent relationship does not exist between the corporation and the promoter.

rules.rule_statement: The corporation can be liable when the corporation adopts the contract. Adoption of a contract can be express or implied. Adoption takes place when the corporation accepts the benefits of the transaction or gives an express acceptance of liability for the debt (e.g., through board resolution after incorporation).

Exception—Liability Upon Contract Adoption
The corporation can be liable when the corporation adopts the contract. Adoption of a contract can be express or implied. Adoption takes place when the corporation accepts the benefits of the transaction or gives an express acceptance of liability for the debt (e.g., through board resolution after incorporation).

rules.rule_statement: An incorporator is a person who signs and files the articles of incorporation with the state. Acting as an incorporator does not engender liability for a contract entered into by a promoter of the corporation. Revised Model Business Corporation Act (RMBCA) § 2.04. See Sivers v. R & F Capital Corp., 858 P.2d 895, 898 (Or. Ct. App. 1993).

rules.rule_statement: Rule To form a corporation, a document, referred to as the “articles of incorporation” or “charter,” must be filed with the state.

Incorporation
Rule To form a corporation, a document, referred to as the “articles of incorporation” or “charter,” must be filed with the state.

rules.rule_statement: The articles of incorporation must include certain basic information about the corporation, such as its name, the number of shares it is authorized to issue, the name and address of its registered agent, and the name and address of each incorporator. The corporation’s name must contain the word “corporation,” “company,” “incorporated,” “limited,” or an abbreviation thereof. The articles of incorporation may include a statement of the corporation’s purposes. A broad statement of that purpose, such as “to engage in any lawful activity,” is acceptable. The RMBCA presumes that each corporation has the broadest lawful purpose unless a more limited purpose is defined in the articles of incorporation. In addition to specifying purposes, the articles of incorporation may enumerate powers that the corporation possesses. Most states automatically grant all corporations broad powers, such as the powers to buy and sell property and to sue and be sued. Some states place restrictions on various corporate actions, including corporate loans to officers and directors. For a corporation with stock listed on a national securities exchange, federal law prohibits the corporation from making personal loans to a director or an executive officer of the corporation. A corporation has perpetual existence unless the articles of incorporation provide otherwise.

rules.rule_statement: The articles of incorporation must be filed with a state official, usually with the secretary of state, and a filing fee paid. Unless a delayed date is specified in the articles, the corporate existence begins when the articles of incorporation are filed. The articles may not set an earlier date than the date of filing. Once the requirements are met, some states treat the corporation as having been formed as of the date of the filing, while other states consider the corporation a legal entity only when the state has accepted the articles of incorporation.

Filing Requirements

Unless a delayed date is specified in the articles, the corporate existence begins when the articles of incorporation are filed. The articles may not set an earlier date than the date of filing. Once the requirements are met, some states treat the corporation as having been formed as of the date of the filing, while other states consider the corporation a legal entity only when the state has accepted the articles of incorporation.

rules.rule_statement: A de jure corporation is created when all the statutory requirements for incorporation have been satisfied. When a de jure corporation is formed, the corporation, rather than persons associated with the corporation (i.e., shareholders, directors, officers, and other employees), is liable for activities undertaken by the corporation.

Effect Of Incorporation—“De Jure” Corporation
A de jure corporation is created when all the statutory requirements for incorporation have been satisfied. When a de jure corporation is formed, the corporation, rather than persons associated with the corporation (i.e., shareholders, directors, officers, and other employees), is liable for activities undertaken by the corporation.

rules.rule_statement: When a person makes an unsuccessful effort to comply with the incorporation requirements, that person may be able to escape personal liability under either the de facto corporation doctrine or the corporation by estoppel doctrine. A de facto corporation is a business entity that is treated as a corporation because the owner has made a good-faith effort to comply with the incorporation requirements and operates the business as a corporation without knowing that these requirements have not been met. The owner of a de facto corporation, as a de facto shareholder, is not personally liable for obligations incurred in the purported corporation’s name. The RMBCA has abolished this doctrine, as have many of the jurisdictions that have adopted the RMBCA. rules.rule_statement: Rule Every corporation is required to have stock that is entitled to vote on matters of corporate governance (e.g., the election of directors to the board) and stock that represents the basic ownership interest in the corporation. "Common stock" are stocks that possess these two characteristics. "Preferred stock" are stocks having preference over other stock to such items as distributions. Traditionally, there are two broad types of securities by which a corporation secures financing for its endeavors: (1) stocks, which carry ownership and control interests in the corporation, and (2) debt securities, which do not. Over time, many securities have been created that blur this distinction. RMBCA § 6.01(b).

rules.rule_statement: When the corporation receives consideration for stock, the stock is deemed fully paid and nonassessable. A shareholder who fails to pay the consideration is liable to the corporation, and any issued stock may be canceled. If stock has not been fully paid, then the corporation or a creditor of the corporation may be able to recover the unpaid amount from the shareholder.

rules.rule_statement: Under the RMBCA, the board of directors must determine that the consideration received for the stock is adequate. Once the board makes that determination, the adequacy of the consideration is not subject to challenge. Par-value stock is stock which is assigned a value. A corporation may, but is not required to, issue par value stock. The par value does not need to be the stock’s market value, and it can even be a nominal amount. However, the corporation is required to receive at least the value assigned to that stock. Watered stock is stock that is issued for consideration less than par value. Because stock is deemed validly issued, paid in full, and nonassessable once the corporation receives adequate consideration (as determined by the board of directors), the RMBCA does not recognize or address the issue of “watered stock.”

However, the corporation is required to receive at least the value assigned to that stock. Watered stock is stock that is issued for consideration less than par value. Because stock is deemed validly issued, paid in full, and nonassessable once the corporation receives adequate consideration (as determined by the board of directors), the RMBCA does not recognize or address the issue of “watered stock.”

rules.rule_statement: Preemptive rights are the rights of a corporation’s shareholders to purchase new shares when the board of directors decides to issue them to maintain their proportional ownership shares in the corporation. Shareholders automatically had preemptive rights at common law, but the RMBCA explicitly precludes preemptive rights unless the articles of incorporation provide otherwise. Example: A and B, the only shareholders of Corporation X, each own 50 shares of stock. The board of directors of Corporation X authorizes the issuance of another 20 shares. With preemptive rights, A and B would each be entitled to purchase 10 additional shares of stock and would retain a 50% ownership interest in the corporation. Without such rights, A or B could become the controlling owner of Corporation X by purchasing at least 11 additional shares of the new stock offering.

Shareholder’S Preemptive Rights
Preemptive rights are the rights of a corporation’s shareholders to purchase new shares when the board of directors decides to issue them to maintain their proportional ownership shares in the corporation. Shareholders automatically had preemptive rights at common law, but the RMBCA explicitly precludes preemptive rights unless the articles of incorporation provide otherwise. Example: A and B, the only shareholders of Corporation X, each own 50 shares of stock. The board of directors of Corporation X authorizes the issuance of another 20 shares. With preemptive rights, A and B would each be entitled to purchase 10 additional shares of stock and would retain a 50% ownership interest in the corporation. Without such rights, A or B could become the controlling owner of Corporation X by purchasing at least 11 additional shares of the new stock offering.

rules.rule_statement: Under the federal Securities Act of 1933, a corporation that issues stock or other securities may be required to register the security with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). In addition to filing the required registration statement with the SEC, the issuer is also required to provide the buyer of the security with a prospectus. The prospectus represents the main part of the registration statement and includes information about the company, its business, and its financial performance.

rules.source_text:
Federal Restrictions—Registration Of Securities
Under the federal Securities Act of 1933, a corporation that issues stock or other securities may be required to register the security with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). In addition to filing the required registration statement with the SEC, the issuer is also required to provide the buyer of the security with a prospectus. The prospectus represents the main part of the registration statement and includes information about the company, its business, and its financial performance.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, registration is required only for public offerings of stocks or securities. Offerings that are considered private, called “private placements,” are exempt from the registration requirements. Private placements include stock sold by a corporation to institutional investors, sophisticated investors, and companies with annual sales of less than $1 million.

rules.rule_statement: A distribution is a transfer of cash or other property from a corporation to one or more of its shareholders. The most common form of distribution is a dividend, which is normally a cash payment made to shareholders. Other forms of distribution include a distribution of indebtedness or a corporation’s purchase of its own stock.
rules.short_rule_statement: A distribution is a transfer of cash or other property from a corporation to one or more of its shareholders. The most common form of distribution is a dividend, which is...
rules.source_text:
Distributions
A distribution is a transfer of cash or other property from a corporation to one or more of its shareholders. The most common form of distribution is a dividend, which is normally a cash payment made to shareholders. Other forms of distribution include a distribution of indebtedness or a corporation’s purchase of its own stock.

rules.rule_statement: The power to authorize a distribution rests with the board of directors. Having authorized a distribution and set sufficient parameters, the board may delegate to a board committee or corporate officer the power to fix the amount and other terms of the distribution. Generally, a shareholder cannot compel the board of directors to authorize a distribution, because that decision is usually discretionary. However, when a board acts in bad faith and abuses its discretion by refusing to declare a distribution, a court may order the board to authorize a distribution.

Authorization By Board Of Directors
The power to authorize a distribution rests with the board of directors. Having authorized a distribution and set sufficient parameters, the board may delegate to a board committee or corporate officer the power to fix the amount and other terms of the distribution. Generally, a shareholder cannot compel the board of directors to authorize a distribution, because that decision is usually discretionary. However, when a board acts in bad faith and abuses its discretion by refusing to declare a distribution, a court may order the board to authorize a distribution.

rules.rule_statement: A corporation may not make a distribution if it is insolvent or if the distribution would cause the corporation to be insolvent.

Limitations On Distributions

rules.rule_statement: A corporation must pass two tests to be deemed solvent and capable of making a distribution: an equity test and a balance-sheet test. Under the equity test, a corporation must be able to pay off its debts as they come due in the usual course of business. Under the balance-sheet test, a corporation’s total assets must exceed its total liabilities plus liquidation preferences of senior securities.

Under the equity test, a corporation must be able to pay off its debts as they come due in the usual course of business. Under the balance-sheet test, a corporation’s total assets must exceed its total liabilities plus liquidation preferences of senior securities.

rules.rule_statement: The right to compel a dividend is a primary and a personal right of the shareholder that can be enforced by a direct action against the corporation. To prevail in a direct action to compel a dividend distribution, a shareholder must prove the existence of (1) funds legally available for the payment of a dividend and (2) bad faith on the part of the directors in their refusal to pay.

rules.rule_statement: Outstanding stock is stock authorized and issued by the corporation. Outstanding stock may be reacquired by the corporation through purchase or redemption (i.e., stock acquired by a forced sale). On repurchase or redemption, that stock constitutes authorized but unissued shares. If the articles of incorporation prohibit the reissuance of stock, then the number of authorized shares is automatically reduced by the number of shares purchased.

Shares Reacquired By Corporation
Outstanding stock is stock authorized and issued by the corporation. Outstanding stock may be reacquired by the corporation through purchase or redemption (i.e., stock acquired by a forced sale). On repurchase or redemption, that stock constitutes authorized but unissued shares. If the articles of incorporation prohibit the reissuance of stock, then the number of authorized shares is automatically reduced by the number of shares purchased.

rules.rule_statement: Distribution of a corporation’s indebtedness, (e.g., bonds or promissory notes), is subject to the same requirements as other distributions. When indebtedness is to be repaid over time (i.e., on an installment basis), the lawfulness of the distribution is tested as of the date of distribution. Corporate indebtedness received as a lawful distribution is on par with a corporation’s general unsecured creditors.

Corporate indebtedness received as a lawful distribution is on par with a corporation’s general unsecured creditors.

rules.rule_statement: Restrictions on the transfer of stock are generally found in closely held corporations. Owners of a closely held corporation often seek to maintain control over the corporation’s business and profits by limiting the number of shareholders in a corporation. This limitation can be accomplished through restrictions on the transferability of shares. Even when a closely held corporation does not restrict the transfer of its stock, a shareholder with a controlling interest in a corporation may have a fiduciary obligation that restricts the transfer of stock to outsiders. See § IV.E.2. Controlling Shareholder, infra.

rules.rule_statement: Stock-transfer restrictions have been subject to challenge as unreasonable restraints on alienation. Of the various forms noted above, the outright prohibition on transfer and the need for prior consent are the most susceptible to attack. However, because the test is one of reasonableness, even these two forms may be justified in particular circumstances, such as when a corporation seeks to preserve its status because it is dependent on the number or identity of its shareholders. Because many stock-transfer restrictions are created through contractual arrangements, they may be subject to contractual defenses. Additionally, the restrictions may be narrowly interpreted and subject to equitable challenges (e.g., abandonment, waiver, or estoppel).

rules.rule_statement: Under the forced-sale doctrine, the forced exchange of shares in a merger or similar transaction constitutes a sale. Interstate commerce must be used in connection with the transaction. Use of a telephone, mail, or email to make the transaction satisfies this requirement, as does the use of a national securities exchange. An in-person transaction may not necessarily satisfy the interstate commerce requirement. A Rule 10b-5 action requires fraudulent or deceptive conduct by the defendant in connection with the sale or purchase of a security. The defendant can engage in this conduct by (1) making an untrue statement of a material fact or (2) failing to state a material fact that is necessary to prevent statements already made from being misleading. Pure omissions are not covered by Rule 10b-5. Instead, the rule only requires the defendant to disclose information when it is necessary to ensure that statements already made by the defendant are accurate and clear. Macquarie Infrastructure Corp. v. Moad Partners, L.P., 601 U.S. 257 (2024).

rules.source_text:
Nondisclosure And Insider Trading
The mere possession of material information that is not public knowledge does not give rise to Rule 10b-5 liability. A person who has insider knowledge does not incur liability unless he also trades stock or other securities based on that knowledge. This is often referred to as the “disclose or abstain” rule.

rules.rule_statement: In establishing that a person has traded based on nonpublic information, a person is presumed to have traded based on the information that he possessed at the time of the trade. An exception exists for trades made in accordance with a preexisting written plan. ii)      Affected traders There are four types of traders who may be liable for failure to disclose information: (1) insiders, (2) constructive insiders, (3) tippees, and (4) misappropriators. An insider is a director, an officer, or other employee of the corporation who uses nonpublic information for personal gain. A constructive insider is a person who has a relationship with the corporation that gives that person access to corporate information not available to the general public (e.g., lawyers, accountants, independent contractors). A tippee is a person who is given information by an insider or a constructive insider (the “tipper”) with the expectation that the information will be used to trade the stock or other securities. The tipper must receive a personal benefit from the disclosure or intend to make a gift to the tippee. To be liable, the tippee must have known (or should have known) that the information was provided to him in violation of the insider’s duty to the corporation. A misappropriator is a person who uses confidential information to trade stock or other securities in violation of the duty of confidentiality owed to the corporation. A defendant’s conduct must involve the misuse of material information. A material fact is a fact which a reasonable investor would find important in deciding whether to purchase or sell a security. A defendant is not strictly liable for making a false or misleading statement or for negligently making such a statement. Instead, the defendant must make the statement intentionally or recklessly. This fault requirement is also known as “scienter.” To maintain a Rule 10b-5 action, a plaintiff must establish that he relied on the defendant’s fraudulent conduct. However, when the defendant’s fraudulent conduct is not aimed directly at the plaintiff (e.g., the defendant issues a press release), then courts have permitted the plaintiff to establish reliance by finding that the defendant’s conduct constituted a fraud on the market.

An insider is a director, an officer, or other employee of the corporation who uses nonpublic information for personal gain. A constructive insider is a person who has a relationship with the corporation that gives that person access to corporate information not available to the general public (e.g., lawyers, accountants, independent contractors). rules.rule_statement: A corporate insider can be forced to return short-swing profits to the corporation through a Section 16(b) action. An insider’s reasons for trading are immaterial. Even an insider who does not possess nonpublic material information must return short?swing profits. A corporate insider is required to report a change in his stock ownership to the SEC to encourage compliance with the short-swing profits rule. Only the following publicly traded corporations are protected by Section 16(b): (1) those that have securities traded on a national securities exchange or (2) those that have assets of more than $10 million and more than 500 shareholders of any class of stock or other equity security. Only corporate directors, officers (e.g., president, vice president, secretary, treasurer, or comptroller), and shareholders who hold more than 10% of any class of stock are subject to a Section 16(b) action. Generally, transactions made before becoming a corporate insider are not considered in determining short-swing profits. However, transactions made after ceasing to be a corporate insider are considered in determining short-swing profits. During any six-month period, a corporate insider who both buys and sells his corporation’s stock is liable to the corporation for any profits made. Profits are computed by matching the highest sale price with the lowest purchase price, then the next highest sale price with the next lowest purchase price, and so on, during the six-month period. Any loss is not taken into account, and all shares are matched with other shares only once. Example: On January 1, President sells 200 shares of ABC Corporation’s stock for $500 each that she purchased several years before for $100 each. On May 1, President purchases 200 shares of stock for $400 each. President has a short-swing profit of $20,000 (i.e., the sale of 200 shares at $500 each, less the purchase of 200 shares at $400 each).

rules.rule_statement: Generally, a corporation maintains a list of shareholders who are entitled to vote (i.e., record owners). A person who is not a record owner may still be entitled to vote. Example: A beneficial owner of the stock may compel the record owner to recognize the beneficial owner’s right to vote. Example: A guardian for an incompetent or a personal representative of a decedent’s estate may compel the corporation to allow her to vote in lieu of the record owner. Voting rights issues may also arise when stock is jointly held. When stock has been subscribed to but not fully paid for, the subscriber’s right to vote the stock may be limited or denied. A corporation is generally not entitled to vote its stock. Stock that has been authorized but not issued by a corporation cannot be voted by the corporation. Similarly, stock that has been authorized and issued by a corporation and then reacquired by the corporation (i.e., treasury stock) cannot be voted. A corporation that owns stock in another corporation generally can vote the stock as any other shareholder can.

rules.rule_statement: When stock is sold or otherwise transferred, an issue may arise as to whether the transferor or the transferee of the stock is entitled to vote at a subsequent shareholders’ meeting. The owner of the stock at the close of business on the record date has the right to vote the stock at the upcoming meeting, regardless of whether the owner subsequently transfers the stock to another person. The transferee of shares after the record date may vote those shares at a scheduled shareholder meeting only by obtaining a proxy to vote the shares from his transferor. Typically, the record date is fixed by the board of directors, although the date can be set by reference to the articles of incorporation or the corporate bylaws and, failing corporate guidance, by statute. The record date can be no more than 70 days prior to the meeting.

A plurality vote requirement for directors means that the individuals with the largest number of votes are elected as directors up to the maximum number of directors to be chosen at the election.

Special Voting For Directors
Corporations may choose directors by cumulative voting if so provided in the articles of incorporation.

rules.rule_statement: When more than one director is to be elected, corporations can allow shareholders to cumulate their votes and cast all those votes for only one (or more than one) of the candidates. The effect of cumulative voting allows minority shareholders to elect representatives to the board. Example: A owns 30 shares of X, Inc. stock. B owns the remaining 70 shares. X, Inc. has three directors. Without cumulative voting, A is unable to elect any of the three directors because B owns a majority of the shares. With cumulative voting, A can elect at least one director by casting all of her 90 votes (i.e., 30 votes per director times three directors) for one director.

X, Inc. has three directors. Without cumulative voting, A is unable to elect any of the three directors because B owns a majority of the shares. With cumulative voting, A can elect at least one director by casting all of her 90 votes (i.e., 30 votes per director times three directors) for one director.

rules.rule_statement: Typically, all directors of the corporation are elected annually. However, some corporations provide for the election of fewer than all the directors, staggering the terms of the directors, which provides for some continuity on the board from election to election. Staggering terms limits the impact of cumulative voting.

rules.rule_statement: A proxy is the authority or power to act for another. A shareholder may vote in person or by proxy. A proxy vote must be executed in a signed writing (or by an electronic transmission) and delivered to the corporation or its agent. A proxy is valid for 11 months unless otherwise specified. A proxy is revocable unless (1) it expressly provides that it is irrevocable and (2) the appointment of the proxy is coupled with an interest. Any act by the shareholder that is inconsistent with a proxy, such as attending a shareholder meeting and voting the shares, revokes the proxy. In the case of multiple proxies given, the last given revokes all previous proxies. Whether a proxy is coupled with an interest depends on whether the proxy holder has (1) a property right in the shares or (2) a security interest given to him to protect him regarding any obligations he incurred or money he advanced. Typically, proxy holders who have a property interest in the shares or a security interest are those who have purchased the shares or otherwise have a business arrangement with the corporation (e.g., a creditor or an employee of the corporation).

rules.rule_statement: A voting pool is a binding voting agreement that shareholders may enter, which provides for the way they will vote their shares. Shareholders retain ownership of their stock under a voting pool agreement. This agreement is a contract that may be specifically enforced. It does not need to be filed with the corporation, and there is no time limit.

rules.source_text:
Voting Pool—Retention Of Legal Ownership
A voting pool is a binding voting agreement that shareholders may enter, which provides for the way they will vote their shares. Shareholders retain ownership of their stock under a voting pool agreement. This agreement is a contract that may be specifically enforced. It does not need to be filed with the corporation, and there is no time limit.

rules.rule_statement: A voting trust is a separate legal entity to which the shareholders’ stock is transferred. Although shareholders who transfer stock to a voting trust retain beneficial ownership of their shares, legal ownership is transferred to the trustee. The trustee votes the shares and distributes the dividends by the terms of the trust. The trustee owes a fiduciary duty to the trust and the beneficial owners of the stock. A voting trust must be in writing, it is limited to 10 years, and the trust instrument must be filed with the corporation.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, shareholders may agree to alter the way a corporation is managed even though the agreement is inconsistent with statutory provisions. Among the matters on which shareholders may agree are: ii) Authorization or making of distributions; iii) Determination of who is a member of the board of directors, the manner of selection or removal of directors, and the terms of office of directors; iv) The exercise or division of voting power by or between the shareholders and directors or by or among any of them, including director proxies; v) A transfer to one or more shareholders or other persons all or part of the authority to exercise corporate powers or to manage the business and affairs of the corporation; and vi) The manner or means by which the exercise of corporate powers or the management of the business and affairs of the corporation is affected. The agreement must be set forth either: i) In the articles of incorporation or the corporate bylaws and approved by all persons who are shareholders at the time of the agreement; or ii) In a written agreement signed by all persons who are shareholders at the time of the agreement and is made known to the corporation. The agreement may be amended only by persons who are shareholders at the time of amendment. Unless otherwise fixed in the agreement, the agreement is valid for 10 years. A person who purchases stock in a corporation with a management agreement without knowledge of the agreement can rescind the purchase agreement. An agreement cannot be entered into with respect to a corporation that has shares listed on a national securities exchange, and such an agreement ceases to be effective for a corporation when its shares are listed on such an exchange. If the agreement limits the discretion or powers of the board of directors, then the directors are relieved of liability for acts or omissions to the extent of the limitation, and the persons in whom such discretion or powers are vested are subject to liability. The existence of the agreement is not a ground for imposing personal liability on shareholders for corporate acts or debts, even though the shareholders, by virtue of the agreement, fail to observe the corporate formalities. RMBCA § 7.32(b). RMBCA § 7.32(c). RMBCA § 7.32(d). RMBCA § 7.32(e)–(f).

rules.rule_statement: For certain corporate records, the shareholder’s demand must be made in good faith and for a proper purpose. The demand must describe with reasonable particularity that purpose and the records to be inspected, and the records must be directly connected to that purpose. A proper purpose is one that relates to the shareholder’s interest in the corporation (e.g., determining the value of one’s shares in a closely held corporation even though the shareholder does not plan to sell the shares). Records not subject to purpose limitation Records subject to purpose limitation Current articles of incorporation and bylaws The financial statements of the corporation Written communications to shareholders Accounting records of the corporation generally made within the past three years Excerpts from minutes of any meeting of, or Minutes of all meetings of, and records of all records of any actions taken without a actions taken without a meeting by, the meeting by, the corporation’s board of corporation’s shareholders directors and board committees List of the names and business addresses A record of the corporation’s current of the corporation’s current directors and shareholders in alphabetical order by class officers or series of shares showing the address of, and the number and class or series of The corporation’s most recent annual report shares held by, each shareholder to the state Improper purposes may include harassment of corporate officials, acquiring corporate secrets, or idle curiosity. Whether a particular document is essential to a stated inspection purpose depends on the context in which the shareholder’s inspection demand arises. The shareholder has the burden of showing credible evidence of improper conduct and must show the documents or records are essential to the stated proper purpose. A shareholder who establishes a proper purpose does not forfeit her inspection rights because she also has an improper purpose. rules.rule_statement: A shareholder may sue the corporation on grounds that do not arise from that person’s status as a shareholder. Example: A shareholder who is struck by a vehicle owned by the corporation and driven by a corporate employee may pursue a negligence claim against the corporation as the injured party of the corporation’s tortious conduct.

rules.rule_statement: A derivative action is an action in which the shareholder sues on behalf of the corporation for harm suffered by the corporation. Although the shareholder also may have suffered harm, recovery generally goes to the corporation. Example: A shareholder brings a derivative action to force a director to disgorge a secret profit earned by the director on a transaction with the corporation. If the shareholder succeeds, the profits recovered will go to the corporation.

rules.rule_statement: In addition to being a shareholder at the time the action is filed and continuing to be a shareholder during the litigation, a plaintiff must also have been a shareholder at the time of the act or omission (or one who receives the shares through a transfer by operation of law from such a shareholder) to bring a derivative action. This is known as the “contemporaneous ownership” rule. The shareholder also must fairly and adequately represent the interests of the corporation. EXAM NOTE: Remember that a creditor of a corporation cannot bring a derivative action.

rules.rule_statement: The plaintiff in a derivative action must make a written demand upon the board of directors to take action. A derivative action may not commence until 90 days have passed from the date of demand. In some states, a demand upon the board is not required if the demand would be futile. Factors for determining futility include whether the directors are disinterested and independent and whether the transaction was the product of a valid exercise of business judgment. Under the RMBCA, however, there is a universal demand requirement for all derivative actions. Therefore, the futility exception is not recognized in states that have adopted the RMBCA. The plaintiff may be excused from waiting a reasonable time for the board to respond to the demand if the delay would result in irreparable injury to the corporation. If the board specifically rejects the demand, then the rejection is tested against the business judgment rule. If there is a business justification for the rejection, then the plaintiff must establish that the board’s rejection was due to a lack of care, loyalty, or good faith to persuade the court to override the board’s refusal.

The limited liability of shareholders is one reason the corporate form is favored as a business entity. The limited liability principle is subject to challenge, primarily concerning one or more individual shareholders of a closely held corporation, and less frequently concerning a corporation that holds a controlling interest in another corporation (i.e., parent-subsidiary relationship).

rules.rule_statement: The factors considered by the courts when piercing the corporate veil include: The initial shareholders fail to supply adequate capital to the corporation considering its business needs (e.g., purchase of equipment, acquisition of facilities from which to operate the business, payment of employees). However, there is no statutory requirement that a corporation have a specific amount of capital to exist. The corporation fails to: issue stock, select directors, hold meetings of the board of directors or the shareholders, appoint officers, keep records, or otherwise comply with statutory requirements. However, a primary purpose of these requirements is to protect shareholders, not to penalize them when they choose to ignore these requirements. The shareholders fail to separate their personal assets from the corporation’s assets (e.g., maintenance of single bank account). This issue arises when third parties are misled into lending funds or advancing credit to the corporation based on a shareholder’s assets. Although limited liability generally protects shareholders from personal liability for corporate debts, a corporate creditor is permitted to protect himself by requiring the shareholder’s personal liability or guarantee before doing business with the corporation. Use of corporate assets for personal purposes (e.g., a company vehicle) may be a factor to justify piercing the corporate veil. However, when use of those assets results in harm to a third party (e.g., through an accident), that tort may result in direct personal liability of the shareholder without piercing the corporate veil. Typically, self-dealing occurs when a shareholder sells to or buys from the corporation goods or other property on terms that are not fair to the corporation. While this action can constitute a breach of a director’s duty of loyalty owed to the corporation, a shareholder does not owe a similar duty to the corporation. However, such conduct by a shareholder may justify piercing the corporate veil. The characterization of siphoning corporate funds implies that the purpose underlying the distribution is to defraud corporate creditors. Therefore, these actions may justify piercing the corporate veil. There is a prohibition on corporate distributions that leave the corporation insolvent and a corresponding provision for recoupment from shareholders (see II.C.3. Director’s liability for unlawful distributions, supra). Recognition of the corporate form undermines a regulatory scheme by permitting a shareholder to escape liability for conduct engaged in by the corporation. Example: A single shareholder may try to escape liability for releasing hazardous chemicals into the environment by claiming that it was a corporate action. This conduct may justify piercing the corporate veil. Not all of the above factors need to be met for the court to pierce the corporate veil, but many of the factors focus on whether the corporation is being used as a “façade” for a dominant shareholder’s personal dealings (i.e., whether the corporation is an “alter ego” or “mere instrumentality” of the shareholder). In the case of two corporations, the focus is whether there is “unity of interest and ownership” between the corporations such that the corporation did not exist independent of the controlling (parent) corporation. However, the failure of a shareholder to respect the corporation as a separate entity is insufficient to justify piercing the corporate veil. Failure must also adversely affect a third party’s ability to recover from the corporation.

rules.rule_statement: A controlling shareholder is a single shareholder—or a group of shareholders acting in concert—that holds a high enough percentage of ownership in a company to enact changes at the highest level. Anyone controlling 50% of a corporation’s shares, plus one, is automatically a controlling shareholder. A much smaller interest can be controlling if the remaining shares are widely dispersed (as in a large, publicly traded corporation) and not actively voted. Additionally, a corporation that requires a two-thirds supermajority of shares to vote in favor of a motion can effectively grant control to a minority shareholder or block of shareholders that owns more than one-third of the shares of the corporation. In some cases, a shareholder can maintain control of a corporation with only 33.4% of the outstanding shares.

Additionally, a corporation that requires a two-thirds supermajority of shares to vote in favor of a motion can effectively grant control to a minority shareholder or block of shareholders that owns more than one-third of the shares of the corporation. Fiduciary Duties
Shareholders do not generally owe fiduciary duties to the corporation or to each other. However, a fiduciary duty to the minority shareholders may arise if the controlling shareholder:

rules.canonical_name: Sells That Interest To An Outsider
rules.rule_statement: ii) Seeks to eliminate other shareholders from the corporation; or iii) Receives a distribution denied to the other shareholders. A controlling shareholder has a duty to disclose to the minority shareholder any information that it knew or should have known if it is information that a reasonable person would consider important in deciding how to vote on a transaction. A controlling shareholder breaches her fiduciary duty to the minority shareholders if nondisclosure causes a loss to the minority shareholders. A loss includes being deprived of a state remedy that would otherwise have been available. When a majority shareholder purchases the interest of the minority, it has a fiduciary duty of fair dealing. The controlling shareholder bears the burden of demonstrating that the process she employed was fair and that the price she selected was fair. Other decisions by a majority shareholder or control group may also be reviewable by a court for good faith and fair dealing toward the minority shareholders under the court’s inherent equity power. However, business dealings between a controlling shareholder and a controlled corporation (e.g., a parent-subsidiary arrangement) that do not involve self-dealing are analyzed using the business judgment standard (See V.H.1.c. Business judgment rule, infra). Weinberger v. UOP, Inc., 457 A.2d 701 (Del. 1983). RMBCA § 10.01, official cmt (citing McNulty v. W. & J. Sloane, 54 N.Y.S.2d 253 (Sup. Ct. 1945)). See Sinclair Oil Corp. v. Levien, 280 A.2d 717 (Del. 1971).

rules.rule_statement: A director who was elected by a particular voting class of stock can be removed only by that same class (or by court proceeding). If cumulative voting is not authorized, then a shareholder vote removes a director if the number of votes for removal exceeds the number of votes against removal. If cumulative voting is authorized, then a director may not be removed if the votes sufficient to elect the director are cast against the director’s removal. A director can also be removed by court proceeding.

rules.rule_statement: The board of directors may hold regular or special meetings. Unless the articles of incorporation or bylaws provide otherwise, a director is entitled to two days’ notice of the date, time, and place of a special meeting, but the purpose is not required. A regular meeting may be held without notice of the date, time, place, or purpose. A director may waive notice of a meeting at any time by a signed written waiver. A director’s attendance also waives notice of that meeting unless the director promptly objects to lack of notice. EXAM NOTE: Unlike a regular meeting of the board of directors, a special meeting of the board of directors requires notice of the date, time and place of the meeting unless waived by a director.  Unlike a special meeting of the shareholders, the notice of a special meeting of the board does not need to state the purpose of the meeting.

rules.rule_statement: ii) The director was not informed to the extent that the director reasonably believed was necessary before making a decision; iii) The director did not show objectivity or independence from the director’s relation to or control by another person having material interest in the challenged conduct; iv) There was a sustained failure by the director to devote attention to an ongoing oversight of the business and affairs of the corporation; v) The director failed to timely investigate a matter of significant material concern after being alerted in a manner that would have caused a reasonably attentive director to do so; or vi) The director received a financial benefit to which he was not entitled, or any other breach of his duties to the corporation. The presumption of good faith afforded by the business judgment rule is overcome if the challenger shows fraud, dereliction of duty, condoning illegal conduct, or a conflict of interest.

Typically, exculpatory provisions do not protect directors from liability for any breach of the duty of loyalty, for acts or omissions that are not in good faith, or for any transactions from which the director received an improper personal benefit.

rules.source_text:
Duty Of Loyalty
A director’s duty of loyalty is the obligation to act in a manner that the director reasonably believes is in the best interests of the corporation. Typically, a director breaches this duty by placing his own interests before those of the corporation.

rules.rule_statement: A director who engages in a conflict-of-interest transaction with his own corporation, also known as “self-dealing,” violates his duty of loyalty unless the transaction is protected under the safe-harbor rule. The business judgment rule does not apply when a director engages in a conflict-of-interest transaction with his corporation. Additionally, a director must not profit at the corporation’s expense. In a parent-subsidiary relationship, a parent corporation may be accused of self-dealing in violation of the duty of loyalty if it engages in a transaction in which the parent receives something from the subsidiary to the exclusion and detriment of the minority shareholders. A conflict-of-interest transaction is any transaction between a director and his corporation that would normally require approval of the board of directors and that is of such financial significance to the director that it would reasonably be expected to influence the director’s vote on the transaction. The interest involved in a conflict-of-interest transaction can be direct or indirect, but it must be financial and material. The standard of determining whether the interest is material is objective and calls on the trier of fact to determine whether a reasonable director in similar circumstances would have been influenced by the financial interest when voting on the matter. Corporate dealings with persons who are related to the director are also subject to conflict-of-interest rules. Related individuals include: the director’s immediate family, parents, siblings, and grandchildren, including the spouses of these individuals, as well as a trust or an estate of which any of those individuals is a substantial beneficiary or the director is a fiduciary. The conflict-of-interest rules can apply to transactions between the corporation and another entity with which the director is associated (e.g., another corporation of which the director is a director, an employee, or an agent or a partnership of which the director is a general partner, an employee, or an agent).

rules.rule_statement: There are three safe harbors by which a conflict-of-interest transaction may enjoy protection: i) Disclosure of all material facts to, and approval by a majority of, the board of directors without a conflicting interest; ii) Disclosure of all material facts to, and approval by a majority of, the votes entitled to be cast by the shareholders without a conflicting interest; and iii) Fairness of the transaction to the corporation at the time of commencement.

Standards For Upholding Transactions
There are three safe harbors by which a conflict-of-interest transaction may enjoy protection: i) Disclosure of all material facts to, and approval by a majority of, the board of directors without a conflicting interest; ii) Disclosure of all material facts to, and approval by a majority of, the votes entitled to be cast by the shareholders without a conflicting interest; and iii) Fairness of the transaction to the corporation at the time of commencement.

rules.rule_statement: The fairness test looks at the substance and procedure of the transaction. Substantively, the court determines whether the corporation received something of comparable value in exchange for what it gave to the director. Procedurally, the court determines whether the process followed by the directors in reaching their decision was appropriate. Interested directors who were on both sides of the transactions in question have the burden of establishing the substantive and procedural fairness of the transactions. Regarding a parent corporation engaged in self-dealing, the main concern under the fairness test is whether the benefit is similar to what might have been obtained in an arm’s-length transaction (i.e., fair price). Procedural fairness is generally not at issue unless there has been a change in control (e.g., a merger between parent and subsidiary).

Fairness Of Transaction
The fairness test looks at the substance and procedure of the transaction. Substantively, the court determines whether the corporation received something of comparable value in exchange for what it gave to the director. Procedurally, the court determines whether the process followed by the directors in reaching their decision was appropriate. Interested directors who were on both sides of the transactions in question have the burden of establishing the substantive and procedural fairness of the transactions. Regarding a parent corporation engaged in self-dealing, the main concern under the fairness test is whether the benefit is similar to what might have been obtained in an arm’s-length transaction (i.e., fair price). Procedural fairness is generally not at issue unless there has been a change in control (e.g., a merger between parent and subsidiary).

rules.rule_statement: Satisfaction of the safe-harbor defenses is not necessarily a complete defense. Some states hold that the burden of proof shifts to the party challenging the transaction to establish that the transaction was unfair to the corporation. A conflict-of-interest transaction that is not protected by a safe?harbor provision may be enjoined or rescinded. Additionally, the corporation may seek damages from the director. Approval of a conflict-of-interest transaction by fully informed disinterested directors triggers the business judgment rule and limits judicial review to issues of gift or waste, with the burden of proof on the party attacking the transaction.

Some states hold that the burden of proof shifts to the party challenging the transaction to establish that the transaction was unfair to the corporation. A conflict-of-interest transaction that is not protected by a safe?harbor provision may be enjoined or rescinded. Additionally, the corporation may seek damages from the director. Approval of a conflict-of-interest transaction by fully informed disinterested directors triggers the business judgment rule and limits judicial review to issues of gift or waste, with the burden of proof on the party attacking the transaction.

rules.rule_statement: In addition to a conflict-of-interest transaction, a director may violate his duty of loyalty by usurping a corporate opportunity rather than first offering the opportunity to the corporation. In determining whether the opportunity is one that must first be offered to the corporation, courts have applied the “interest or expectancy” test or the “line of business” test.

Usurpation Of Corporate Opportunity
In addition to a conflict-of-interest transaction, a director may violate his duty of loyalty by usurping a corporate opportunity rather than first offering the opportunity to the corporation. In determining whether the opportunity is one that must first be offered to the corporation, courts have applied the “interest or expectancy” test or the “line of business” test.

rules.rule_statement: Under the “interest or expectancy” test, the key is whether the corporation has an existing interest (e.g., an option to buy) or an expectancy arising from an existing right (e.g., purchase of property currently leased) in the opportunity. An expectancy can also exist when the corporation is actively seeking a similar opportunity.

An expectancy can also exist when the corporation is actively seeking a similar opportunity.

rules.rule_statement: Under the broader “line of business” test, the key is whether the opportunity is within the corporation’s current or prospective line of business. Whether an opportunity satisfies this test frequently depends on how expansively the corporation’s line of business is characterized.

rules.rule_statement: A corporation may indemnify a director in the unsuccessful defense of a suit when: i) The director acted in good faith with the reasonable belief that his conduct was in the best interests of the corporation, or that his conduct was at least not opposed to the best interests of the corporation; and ii) In the case of a criminal proceeding, the director did not have reasonable cause to believe that his conduct was unlawful. Indemnification can extend to liability as well as expenses when the action is brought by a third party, but only to expenses if the action is brought by or on behalf of the corporation. The authorization for permissive indemnification requires the approval of a disinterested majority of directors or shareholders or an independent attorney chosen by disinterested directors.

rules.rule_statement: Typically, a corporation’s officers are composed of a president, secretary, and treasurer. An individual may hold more than one office, but some states prohibit a person from serving dual roles when the officers serve as a check on each other. The RMBCA does not specify which officers a corporation must have; it only provides that the corporate bylaws delineate the officers of the corporation. RMBCA § 8.40.

rules.rule_statement: An officer’s authority can be actual, implied, or apparent. Actual authority wielded by an officer is defined by the corporate bylaws or set by the board of directors. An officer has implied authority to perform tasks necessary to carry out the officer’s duties by virtue of her status or position, so long as the matter is within the scope of ordinary business. However, the officer does not have the authority to bind the corporation by extraordinary acts. In determining whether a transaction is extraordinary, the court might consider the economic magnitude of the action in relation to corporate earnings and assets, the extent of the risk involved, the time span of the action’s effect, and the cost of reversing the action. An officer has apparent authority if the corporation holds the officer out as having authority to bind the corporation to third parties.

However, the officer does not have the authority to bind the corporation by extraordinary acts. Moreover, all employees, as agents of the corporation, owe the corporation these duties of care and loyalty.

rules.rule_statement: The chief executive officer (CEO) and chief financial officer (CFO) of a publicly traded corporation must certify the accuracy of the corporation’s financial reports that are filed with the SEC. In addition to facing criminal penalties for filing a false report, when financial reports must be restated because of such misconduct, a CEO and a CFO must forfeit incentive-based pay and must return profits from stock sales for one year. RMBCA § 8.41. RMBCA § 8.42. Sarbanes-Oxley Act 302. Sarbanes-Oxley Act 304.

rules.rule_statement: As an agent of the corporation, an officer does not incur liability to third parties merely for the performance of duties for the corporation. However, an officer can be liable to a third party if the officer has acted in his personal capacity (e.g., guaranteed a corporate loan) or has engaged in purposeful tortious behavior.

rules.rule_statement: An officer is entitled to indemnification to the same extent and subject to the same restrictions as a corporate director. Similar insurance rules apply as well.

rules.rule_statement: An officer may be removed at any time, with or without cause. The existence of an employment contract between an officer and the corporation does not prevent the removal of the officer, but it may give rise to contractual remedies, such as damages, if removal constitutes a breach of contract. RMBCA § 8.43.

rules.rule_statement: An employee who is not an officer is an agent of the corporation and is subject to the responsibilities of an agent. In turn, the employee is owed duties by the corporate principal. As an agent, the employee can act on behalf of the corporation to the extent of the employee’s authority and is usually protected as an agent from liability for actions undertaken by that authority.

rules.rule_statement: A merger is the combination of two or more corporations so that only one corporation survives. The surviving corporation may be created as a result of the merger, rather than existing before the merger. This process is referred to as a “consolidation.”

Mergers
A merger is the combination of two or more corporations so that only one corporation survives. The surviving corporation may be created as a result of the merger, rather than existing before the merger. This process is referred to as a “consolidation.”

rules.rule_statement: Shareholder approval requires a majority vote, meaning a majority of the votes cast, but the shareholders’ meeting at which the vote is taken is subject to a quorum requirement, which is usually a majority of shares entitled to vote. If the corporation has more than one class of stock, and the amendment would affect the rights of a particular class of stock, then the holders of that class of stock must also approve of the amendment.

If the corporation has more than one class of stock, and the amendment would affect the rights of a particular class of stock, then the holders of that class of stock must also approve of the amendment.

rules.rule_statement: A merger between a parent corporation and a subsidiary corporation may occur without the approval of the subsidiary when the parent owns at least 90% of the voting power of each class of outstanding stock of the subsidiary. A parent corporation may also effect a merger between two 90% or more owned subsidiary corporations without the approval of the shareholders of either corporation. In all these mergers, approval by a subsidiary’s board of directors is also not required.

Parent-Subsidiary Merger
A merger between a parent corporation and a subsidiary corporation may occur without the approval of the subsidiary when the parent owns at least 90% of the voting power of each class of outstanding stock of the subsidiary. A parent corporation may also effect a merger between two 90% or more owned subsidiary corporations without the approval of the shareholders of either corporation. In all these mergers, approval by a subsidiary’s board of directors is also not required.

rules.rule_statement: A minnow-whale merger is a merger of a small corporation (i.e., a "minnow") into a large corporation (i.e., a "whale"). This type of merger may not require approval of the shareholders of the surviving large corporation. Approval is not required if: i) The merger cannot result in an increase of more than 20% in the voting power of the outstanding stock of the surviving corporation; ii) The articles of incorporation of the surviving corporation will not differ from the articles before the merger; and iii) The premerger shareholders of the surviving corporation are otherwise unaffected by the merger.

Minnow-Whale Merger
A minnow-whale merger is a merger of a small corporation (i.e., a "minnow") into a large corporation (i.e., a "whale"). This type of merger may not require approval of the shareholders of the surviving large corporation. Approval is not required if: i) The merger cannot result in an increase of more than 20% in the voting power of the outstanding stock of the surviving corporation; ii) The articles of incorporation of the surviving corporation will not differ from the articles before the merger; and iii) The premerger shareholders of the surviving corporation are otherwise unaffected by the merger.

rules.rule_statement: All assets and liabilities owned by a corporation that are merged into another corporation are then owned by the surviving corporation after the merger.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A corporation may acquire stock in another corporation and secure control of that corporation without going through the process of effecting a statutory merger. The two primary means by which a corporation can acquire stock in another corporation is by exchanging its own stock for that stock or by paying cash or other property for the stock.

Stock Acquisition

The two primary means by which a corporation can acquire stock in another corporation is by exchanging its own stock for that stock or by paying cash or other property for the stock.

rules.rule_statement: A corporation may offer its own stock to shareholders in another corporation in exchange for their stock in that corporation (i.e., a stock swap). Generally, a shareholder in the other corporation may retain his stock and not participate in the stock swap. However, the RMBCA sets out a procedure, labeled a “share exchange,” which parallels the procedure for a merger. This procedure requires all shareholders to participate in the stock swap. Dissenting shareholders are given the right of appraisal in a stock swap.

Stock-For-Stock Exchange
A corporation may offer its own stock to shareholders in another corporation in exchange for their stock in that corporation (i.e., a stock swap). Generally, a shareholder in the other corporation may retain his stock and not participate in the stock swap. However, the RMBCA sets out a procedure, labeled a “share exchange,” which parallels the procedure for a merger. This procedure requires all shareholders to participate in the stock swap. Dissenting shareholders are given the right of appraisal in a stock swap.

rules.rule_statement: A corporation may (1) purchase stock in another corporation on the open market or (2) make an offer to buy the stock from the current shareholders (i.e., a tender offer). RMBCA § 11.03.

rules.rule_statement: A shareholder who is entitled to vote on a merger, an acquisition, or an amendment of the corporation’s articles of incorporation has appraisal rights. A minority shareholder in a short-form merger can exercise appraisal rights, even though the shareholder cannot vote on the merger. However, if a shareholder can sell his stock in a market that is both liquid and reliable, such as the New York Stock Exchange or the American Stock Exchange, the shareholder does not have a right of appraisal, because the market is providing him with the opportunity to sell his stock at its fair value.

However, if a shareholder can sell his stock in a market that is both liquid and reliable, such as the New York Stock Exchange or the American Stock Exchange, the shareholder does not have a right of appraisal, because the market is providing him with the opportunity to sell his stock at its fair value.

rules.rule_statement: To exercise the right of appraisal, a shareholder must send a written notice to the corporation of her intent to do so. This notice must be delivered to the corporation before the shareholders vote on the proposed action.

If the corporation and the shareholder exercising the right of appraisal do not agree on a price for the shareholder’s stock, then the fair value of the stock is determined through a court action.

rules.rule_statement: A dissolved corporation may continue to exist as a corporation for the limited purpose of winding up its affairs and liquidating its business. This includes: (1) collecting assets, (2) disposing of property that will not be distributed to shareholders, (3) discharging liabilities, and (4) distributing property among shareholders according to their interests. Winding up does not include: (1) transferring title to the corporation’s property, (2) preventing transfer of shares or securities, (3) changing quorum or voting requirements, (4) terminating the authority of the registered corporate agent, or (5) preventing commencement of a proceeding by or against the corporation.

rules.source_text:
Effect Of Dissolution—Winding Up
A dissolved corporation may continue to exist as a corporation for the limited purpose of winding up its affairs and liquidating its business. This includes: (1) collecting assets, (2) disposing of property that will not be distributed to shareholders, (3) discharging liabilities, and (4) distributing property among shareholders according to their interests. Winding up does not include: (1) transferring title to the corporation’s property, (2) preventing transfer of shares or securities, (3) changing quorum or voting requirements, (4) terminating the authority of the registered corporate agent, or (5) preventing commencement of a proceeding by or against the corporation.

rules.rule_statement: The directors of a corporation are responsible for distribution of the corporate assets and may be liable for improper distributions. The assets must be distributed in the following order: i) To creditors of the corporation to pay the debts and other obligations of the corporation, including bona fide obligations owed to shareholders; ii) To shareholders of stock with preferences in liquidation; and iii) To shareholders of other stock. Regarding item (i), above, under the Deep Rock doctrine, when factors exist that would permit the corporate veil to be pierced (see IV.E.1. Piercing the corporate veil, supra), amounts owed by the corporation to a shareholder may be subordinated to those owed to third-party creditors. The same circumstances can also give rise to the same result on grounds of equitable subordination. RMBCA § 14.01. RMBCA § 14.02. RMBCA § 14.05. RMBCA § 14.05. Taylor v. Standard Gas and Electric Co. (Deep Rock), 306 U.S. 307 (1939).

rules.rule_statement: The doctrine of shareholder oppression protects the minority from the improper exercise of majority control. Recent cases have made involuntary dissolution easier by interpreting statutory provisions regarding involuntary dissolution to refer to protecting the reasonable expectations of the shareholders in the corporation. Other courts have indicated that dissolution should be considered in light of the feasibility of protecting the expectations the parties originally had regarding agreements about various forms of compensation.

Oppression Doctrine
The doctrine of shareholder oppression protects the minority from the improper exercise of majority control. Recent cases have made involuntary dissolution easier by interpreting statutory provisions regarding involuntary dissolution to refer to protecting the reasonable expectations of the shareholders in the corporation. Other courts have indicated that dissolution should be considered in light of the feasibility of protecting the expectations the parties originally had regarding agreements about various forms of compensation.

rules.rule_statement: Upon the petitioner’s establishment of the necessary grounds, the court may dissolve the corporation. The court has equitable powers to issue injunctions, appoint a receiver, and take other steps necessary to preserve the corporation’s assets. If the court orders the dissolution of the corporation, then distribution of the corporation’s assets generally adheres to that of a voluntary distribution unless equity requires otherwise. rules.rule_statement: The state may force a corporation to forfeit its right to exist or administratively dissolve the corporation if the corporation (1) failed to pay fees or taxes, (2) failed to file required reports or notices, or (3) abused its powers. Continuing to operate as a corporation after forfeiture can result in the personal liability of the operators. RMBCA §§ 14.20–14.21, 14.30(a).

Continuing to operate as a corporation after forfeiture can result in the personal liability of the operators. RMBCA §§ 14.20–14.21, 14.30(a).

rules.rule_statement: A closely held corporation is a corporation with only a few shareholders and a more relaxed style of governance. Closely Held And Close Corporations
A closely held corporation is a corporation with only a few shareholders and a more relaxed style of governance. The terms “closely held corporation” and “close corporation” are frequently used interchangeably. Shareholders often serve as both directors and officers of the corporation. Stock of the corporation is not publicly traded, and many states allow shareholders to do away with many of the corporate formalities.

rules.rule_statement: A foreign corporation is a corporation that is incorporated in another state. To do business in a state other than its state of incorporation, a foreign corporation is required to register with that state and receive a “certificate of authority.” Failure to do so prevents the foreign corporation from suing, but not from being sued, in state courts until registered. However, it does not impair the validity of corporate acts or contracts or prevent the corporation from defending any proceeding within the state. The law of the jurisdiction of formation of a foreign corporation governs the internal affairs of the foreign corporation and the interest holder liability of its shareholders. A foreign corporation is not precluded from registering to do business in a state because of any difference between the law of the foreign corporation’s jurisdiction of formation and the law of the state in which it seeks to do business. Actions like holding board meetings, maintaining bank accounts, and selling through independent contractors, do not constitute doing business within a state. RMBCA §§ 15.01–15.32.

A shareholder in a professional corporation must be a member of the applicable profession. A professional corporation does not shield an employee from liability arising from her own malpractice. However, it may provide protection against vicarious liability arising from malpractice by other professionals in the corporation.

rules.rule_statement: A corporation is usually subject to tax as a “C corporation,” which is a separate taxable entity from its shareholders, causing the corporation to face double taxation. The corporation pays taxes first on profits and again as shareholders on distributions received from the corporation. However, a corporation may elect to avoid double taxation as an “S corporation.” An S corporation is a corporation in which the income and expenses of the corporation are passed through to shareholders (who are then taxed on those items directly). To become an S corporation for federal tax purposes, a corporation must file Internal Revenue Service (IRS) Form 2553, and the IRS must approve the application. Companies that file as S corporations can have no more than 100 shareholders. Only individuals, estates, certain exempt organizations, or certain trusts may be shareholders, and the shareholders must all be either U.S. citizens or resident aliens (nonresident aliens are not permitted). The S corporation may not have more than one class of stock. Each shareholder must consent to the S corporation election for a corporation to become an S corporation. 26 U.S.C. § 1361, et seq.

rules.rule_statement: A benefit corporation is a for-profit entity with the corporate purpose of creating a social or environmental benefit. Many states have enacted benefit corporation acts. The purposes of such corporations may include providing services to low-income or underserved individuals or communities, improving human health, preserving the environment, and promoting the arts, sciences, or advancement of knowledge. Directors are shielded from liability for making business decisions aimed towards the defined social or environmental purpose, even when the decisions negatively impact shareholder profit.

rules.rule_statement: A limited liability company (LLC) is a legally recognized business entity distinct from its members that enjoys the pass-through tax advantage of a partnership and the limited liability of a corporation. An LLC also provides flexibility in managing the entity. All 50 states have adopted the Revised Uniform Limited Liability Company Act (RULLCA). Many of the rules applicable to corporations and corporate governance have counterparts applicable to LLCs.

rules.rule_statement: The articles (certificate) of organization is the document that must be filed with the state to create an LLC. The articles of organization merely reflects the existence of an LLC. It must include the LLC’s name, mailing address, and, if there are no members upon filing, a statement to that effect. The LLC does not come into existence until at least one person becomes a member of the LLC. This document is different from articles of incorporation, a document that has a substantially greater power to affect the rules for the corporate entity and its owners.

Good-faith effort to incorporate, supra.) when a good faith effort was made to file articles of organization together with the operation of the business as an LLC. Compare P.D. 2000, L.L.C. v. First Fin. Planners, Inc., 998 S.W.2d 108 (Mo. Ct. App. 1999) (applying estoppel to prevent a party from avoiding a contract it had entered into with the LLC prior to LLC formation), with Colo. Rev. Stat. § 7-80-105 (“All persons who assume to act as a limited liability company without authority to do so and without good-faith belief that they have such authority shall be jointly and severally liable for all debts and liabilities incurred by such persons so acting.”).

rules.rule_statement: An operating agreement is a document that an LLC may adopt to govern any or all aspects of its business. The operating agreement generally takes precedence over contrary statutory provisions, and statutory default provisions apply when the operating agreement is silent on an issue. An operating agreement is considered a contract that is governed by contract principles of law.
