rules.rule_statement: Unless the members otherwise agree, most LLC statutes provide that a member cannot transfer her LLC interest unless all members consent. The transfer of a membership interest to another person does not automatically give that person the right to participate in the management of the LLC. Instead, the transferee merely acquires the transferor’s right to share in the LLC’s profits and losses. Some LLC statutes permit the articles of organization to provide standing consent for new members. Many statutes also permit transfer of financial rights to creditors, who can obtain a charging order against the member’s interest. A charging order constitutes a lien on a judgment debtor’s transferable interest and requires the LLC to pay to the judgment debtor any distribution that otherwise would be paid to the member. A charging order is the sole method by which a judgment creditor of a member or transferee can extract any value from a member’s ownership interest in an LLC. The operating agreement generally has no power to alter the provisions of this section to the prejudice of third parties.

rules.rule_statement: A member may dissociate from an LLC at any time by communicating the intent to withdraw. Dissociation does not automatically trigger dissolution of the LLC nor does it give the member a right to receive payment for the member’s LLC interest absent a provision to the contrary in the operating agreement. The LLC may elect to liquidate the fair value of that person’s interests, as of the date the person ceased to be a member, based on the person’s right to share in distributions from the LLC. The continuing members of the LLC, following the dissociation of a member, will be deemed to have entered into an operating agreement in effect immediately prior to the dissociation. Members bound by the operating agreement are only those members who have not withdrawn.

The continuing members of the LLC, following the dissociation of a member, will be deemed to have entered into an operating agreement in effect immediately prior to the dissociation. Members bound by the operating agreement are only those members who have not withdrawn.

rules.rule_statement: An LLC can be (1) member-managed (direct management of the LLC by its members) or (2) manager-managed (centralized management of the LLC by one or more managers who are not required to be members). Unless the operating agreement or certificate of incorporation provides otherwise, the default management arrangement is member-management. However, a manager- managed LLC that does not designate a manager is still manager-managed with the manager position vacant.

rules.rule_statement: A member of an LLC is generally not liable as a member for an LLC’s obligations. If a member renders professional services in an LLC, the member, as well as the LLC, may be liable for torts committed while rendering those services. A manager or a managing member of an LLC is not personally liable for obligations incurred on behalf of the LLC. Members of a manager-managed LLC do not have the right to maintain a direct action against the manager of the LLC when the alleged misconduct caused harm only to the LLC.

rules.rule_statement: A member’s duty of care is subject to the business judgment rule. This duty requires the member to act with the care that a person in a like position would reasonably exercise under similar circumstances and in a manner the member reasonably believes to be in the best interests of the company. Though members in a member-managed LLC or managers of a manager-managed LLC owe a duty of care to the LLC, they are not liable for simple negligence. The duty of care consists of refraining from engaging in: (1) grossly negligent conduct, (2) reckless conduct, (3) intentional misconduct, or (4) a knowing violation of law. Some state statutes reject the “gross negligence” standard and impose an ordinary negligence standard when determining breaches of a duty of care. In these states, the business judgment rule may apply to protect LLC managers and members from liability when decisions are made in good faith.

rules.rule_statement: A member can withdraw or dissociate at any time and without reason, even if doing so violates the operating agreement, by providing notice to the LLC. Written notice is not required under the ULLCA. Dissociation does not discharge the member’s interest or liability and does not necessarily trigger dissolution and winding up. By dissociating, the member relinquishes his right to participate in the LLC. Unlike a partner in a partnership, the dissociated member is not entitled to receive payment for the member’s pro rata share of the LLC’s net assets. Instead, the dissociated member, like a minority shareholder, remains entitled to receive distributions authorized by the LLC. However, the disassociated member cannot force the LLC to make payments to him, unless the operating agreement provides otherwise. ULLCA § 407(c). See RULLCA § 304(b) (2006). rules.rule_statement: Members may bring direct actions against the LLC and other members to enforce their rights as members under the operating agreement and the state LLC statute. To do so, a member must plead and prove an actual or threatened injury, which is not solely the result of an injury suffered by the LLC. Although a member may sue the LLC or another member to enforce the member’s rights under the operating agreement, members of a manager-managed LLC do not have the right to maintain a direct action against the manager of the LLC when the alleged misconduct caused harm only to the LLC.

Direct Suits
Members may bring direct actions against the LLC and other members to enforce their rights as members under the operating agreement and the state LLC statute. To do so, a member must plead and prove an actual or threatened injury, which is not solely the result of an injury suffered by the LLC. Although a member may sue the LLC or another member to enforce the member’s rights under the operating agreement, members of a manager-managed LLC do not have the right to maintain a direct action against the manager of the LLC when the alleged misconduct caused harm only to the LLC.

rules.rule_statement: Members may bring a derivative action on behalf of the LLC to enforce its rights. A demand must be made on the other members or manager to initiate the action, unless the demand would be futile. In their complaint, members must also state the efforts they made to prompt the manager's action or provide reasons for not doing so. ULLCA § 801. See also Wright v. Herman, 230 F.R.D. 1, 10 (2005). ULLCA § 802. ULLCA § 804.

Derivative Suits

In their complaint, members must also state the efforts they made to prompt the manager's action or provide reasons for not doing so. ULLCA § 801. See also Wright v. Herman, 230 F.R.D. 1, 10 (2005). ULLCA § 802. ULLCA § 804.

rules.rule_statement: Many recent LLC statutes do not limit the duration of LLCs. Duration is perpetual unless the operating agreement provides otherwise. Like other business entities, an LLC may merge with another LLC or another business entity (e.g., partnership, corporation). Under the ULLCA, dissolution and dissociation are not the same. A member may dissociate from the LLC at any time by expressing a desire to withdraw. Dissociation does not result in dissolution.

rules.source_text:
Dissolution
Many recent LLC statutes do not limit the duration of LLCs. Duration is perpetual unless the operating agreement provides otherwise. Like other business entities, an LLC may merge with another LLC or another business entity (e.g., partnership, corporation). Under the ULLCA, dissolution and dissociation are not the same. A member may dissociate from the LLC at any time by expressing a desire to withdraw. Dissociation does not result in dissolution.

rules.rule_statement: Some LLC statutes allow a member to seek a court order for involuntary dissolution if a controlling member acts in a way that is oppressive and directly harmful to the member seeking the order. Whether the member is successful depends on whether the action violated the member’s reasonable expectations. Unless the operating agreement provides otherwise, the court may also order a remedy other than dissolution. Some states allow LLC members to contractually waive their right to petition for judicial dissolution.

Involuntary Dissolution
Some LLC statutes allow a member to seek a court order for involuntary dissolution if a controlling member acts in a way that is oppressive and directly harmful to the member seeking the order. Whether the member is successful depends on whether the action violated the member’s reasonable expectations. Unless the operating agreement provides otherwise, the court may also order a remedy other than dissolution. Some states allow LLC members to contractually waive their right to petition for judicial dissolution.

Claimants have at least 120 days after receipt of the notice to file a claim. A claim that has been timely asserted may be enforced against a dissolved LLC to the extent of its undistributed assets. If the assets have been distributed after dissolution, the claim may be enforced against a member or transferee to the extent of that person’s proportionate share of the claim or of the LLC’s assets distributed to the member or transferee.
